title: Growth and occurrence of Spruce and Fir on pulpwood lands in Northern Michigan.
abstract: The objects of this study, which was initiated in 1933, were (1) to devise methods useful in determining site qualities of land bearing pulpwood stands of various types; (2) to compile growth tables and show how they could be used in predicting future yields of various types of stand; (3) to determine the rate of diameter and height growth of the different species occurring on pulpwood lands; (4) to observe changes in stand composition as a result of cutting; (5) to acquire information on losses due to logging, wind, insects and disease; (6) to note conditions favourable or unfavourable to the establishment of reproduction. Site quality, diameter, and height growth were separately calculated and are presented in tables, for the even-aged, uneven-aged, overtopped, and cut-over stands. Site qualities for even-aged stands were found by the use of age-height relationships of dominant trees on a site-index chart. For the other kinds of stand, the soil type method is recommended. The volume growth of fully stocked, even-aged stands is very irregular. On fair to good sites, young stands, 30-40 years of age, might produce more than 1 cord per acre per year, decreasing to little more than 0.1 cord as the stand approaches 100 years of age. The highest average annual growth is obtained when stands reach 60 years of age, and even-aged stands might well be cut at this age, especially if they contain a large proportion of Balsam Fir, which deteriorates very rapidly. Uneven-aged stands grow much more uniformly from year to year; fully stocked stands on good sites may grow as much as 1 cord per acre per year for limited periods; on poor sites the growth would not exceed 0.5 cord. Over-topped stands of Spruce and Fir are usually found on the better soils. Growth of the understorey is slowed up considerably when the overstorey is dense (75-100 per cent.), but not very much where the overstorey is moderate (50-75 per cent.). A fairly well stocked understorey of Spruce and Fir on a fair site may produce from 0.5 to 0.7 cord of pulpwood per acre per year under a moderate canopy. Under a full canopy the growth would be less than 0.4 cord. Cut-over stands that have been burned over after logging generally revert to Aspen. Aspen outgrows and generally outnumbers all other common species found on Spruce/Fir cut-over lands. Spruce and Fir grow very slowly in youth and therefore suffer various degrees of suppression from Aspen and other hardwoods; if liberated, however, they respond with height and diameter growth second only to Aspen. One of the main problems in providing for a succeeding stand of Spruce and Fir is their susceptibility to windthrow. Losses from this cause generally range between 40 and 70 per cent. of the residual volume in heavily cut stands and may approach 100 per cent. in some. The most important factors affecting the kind and quantity of reproduction following cutting are: fire, proximity of seed trees, seedbed conditions, degree of cutting, vegetative cover, logging damage, and slash. Reproduction of pulpwood species is generally adequate on cut-over areas if fire is excluded, otherwise it is sparse or non-existent. Spruce and Fir prefer a mineral seedbed to one of duff for regeneration in quantity, but as Aspen also prefers it, they do not gain control of such sites, but have a better chance where there is moderate shade over a duff layer: such conditions result from light or moderate cutting. Heavy cutting destroys much of the advance reproduction. Even where it is carefully done, 15-20 per cent. of the reproduction is lost. Lack of slash disposal on most logging operations frequently leaves the area unproductive for 15-20 years. title: Symposium on entomological problems. Simposium oor entomologiese probleme. 17-21 July/Julie 1961.
abstract:
Papers of entomological interest presented at a symposium held in South Africa in 1961 included the following:
ETHELSTON (J. D.). Investigations of the ecology, biology and control of Ephestia elutella Hbn. in stored semi-mature Turkish tobacco (pp. 1-5). Ephestia elutella (Hb.) causes considerable damage to Turkish tobacco produced on farms in the Western Cape Province, where the crop is picked in November-February and stored for about four months before baling and dispatch. Studies showed that the spring-brood adults emerge in September-January, whereas the total moth-emergence period extends to April. In rearing tests, 3 1/2 generations were reared at 80°F. and 75 per cent. relative humidity with an average developmental period from egg to adult of 46 days; this would permit 5 1/4 generations a year. Development lasted 81 days at room temperature and 75 per cent. relative humidity, with considerable variations in duration, and 105 days at room temperature and room humidity, when all the larvae died after 1 1/2 generations. A space spray of pyrethrins and piperonyl butoxide gave complete mortality of adults when applied twice a week, and fumigation of tobacco stacks with methyl bromide killed all the larvae.
BARNARD (A.). Bestryding van die koringluis [Control of the grain aphid] (pp. 6-11). The grain aphid [Toxoptera graminum[Schizaphis graminum] (Rond.)] is a serious pest of wheat in South Africa, particularly in the Orange Free State, where an outbreak occurred in 1957-58. Only alate and apterous parthenogenetic females occur, and sexual forms are not produced. Considerable control is afforded by parasites of the genus Aphidius and Coccinellids. Various insecticides were tested during the outbreak, and sprays of methyl-demeton (Metasystox) are recommended. Others also gave good results.
BOYES (D. G.). The bionomics and control of Mirids attacking castor (pp. 12-15). Castor [Ricinus communis] is being grown in South Africa. It is attacked by the Noctuid Achaea catella Gn., which can be controlled by spraying with DDT or endrin, and by Mirids. The latter include Lygus ricini Tayl. and species of Eurystylus, the identity of which is obscure. Lygus attacks the young leaves and shoots, and Eury stylus, which is much more important, the inflorescences. The bionomics of these insects are described. In tests of sprays for control, the best results as regards number of Mirids per male inflorescence and yield were given by isobenzan (W.L.1650), followed by DDT, endosulfan (Thiodan [endosulfan]) and endrin, which were about equal. Slight control is afforded by Onus (Triphleps) thripoborus (Hesse), which preys on the first-instar nymphs.
BROODRYK (S. W.). Die insekteplae van katoen [The insect pests of cotton] (pp. 16-19). The most injurious insect pests of cotton in South Africa are Heliothis armigera[Helicoverpa armigera] (Hb.), Diparopsis castanea[Diparopsis watersi] Hmps., Earias spp., Dysdercus spp. and sometimes Syagrus rugifrons Baly. Notes are given on their bionomics and control. Pectinophora (Platyedrd) gossypiella (Saund.) occurs in the Sabi Valley of Rhodesia [48 497], and it is to be hoped that the quarantine measures in force will prevent its crossing the border into South Africa.
QUEDNAU (F. W.). Some aspects of investigations on parasites of the Citrus red scale (pp. 20-23). Aonidiella aurantii (Mask.) is the most important pest of Citrus in South Africa. Parasites of the genus Aphytis are reared and liberated for its control in California, and progress towards a similar programme in South Africa is reviewed. The main species present is apparently an undescribed one, with two biological forms, and laboratory investigations on it have been begun.
HARTWIG (E. K.). Wingerd-filloksera op ongeente sultanastokke langs die Benede-Oranje [Vine Phylloxera on ungrafted sultana vines on the Benede-Orange River] (pp. 24-28). Phylloxera vitifoliae (Fitch) (Dactylosphaera vitifoliae (Shimer)) occurs in South Africa, but only the root form is injurious. It has caused damage to vines grown for the production of sultanas in the Benede-Orange region. Control by grafting on American resistant stocks, by the use of soil insecticides and by planting in sandy soil is discussed.
BOT (J.). Beyolkingstudie van sekere skadelike en nuttige insekte in die vrugte-boord [Population studies of harmful and useful insects in fruit growing] (pp. 30-36, 8 figs.). Studies on the population dynamics of various injurious and beneficial orchard insects were carried out at a place near Pretoria. Bait-sprays and bait-pots were used, and the results are illustrated by reference to various fruit-flies and insect predators and parasites.
KRIEGLER (P. J.). Die huidige status van boordmyte op sagtevrugte in Wes-Kaapland [The present-day status of agricultural mites on fruit in the Western Cape Province] (pp. 37-42). The phytophagous mites of most importance on fruit trees in the Western Cape Province are Tetranychus cinnabarinus (Boisd.) [cf. R.A.E., A 52 173], Bryobia praetiosa Koch, and (on pear) Eriophyes pyri (Pgst.). Aculus cornutus (Banks) and (on vines) E. vitis (Pgst.) are less injurious. Recent increases in populations of these mites and their causes, including the use of insecticides [cf. 48 328], and suitable remedies are described.
WEBB (D. v. V.). Preliminary investigations on, the biology, ecology and control of the Emperor moths, Nudaurelia cytherea capensis, Stoll., and Gonimbrasia tyrrhea, Cr., attacking trees and shrubs in the Western and South-Western Cape Province (pp. 44-51, 6 refs.). Nudaurelia cytherea capensis (Cram.) causes serious damage to plantations and windbreaks of Pinus radiata and indigenous shrubs used for reclamation in the Western Cape Province, where heavy and sustained outbreaks occur, and Gonimbrasia tyrrhea (Cram.) is injurious to gray poplar (Populus canescens) in an area in the south-west (Langkloof, District George), where the larvae migrate from these trees to apple orchards, which they defoliate; they also defoliate Acacia karroo, which supplies fodder for goats and cattle, in Namakwaland and other trees and shrubs along the Berg River, in the Hopefield District. Research on the bionomics, ecology and control of these Saturniids was carried out in 1956-61, and the results are described, particularly those on parasites and aerial spraying. Both moths have one generation a year and fly predominantly in winter and early spring, the summer and autumn being passed by the pupae in diapause, although G. tyrrhea may have a non-diapause strain producing two generations a year. The principal agent of biological control is Mesocomys pulchriceps Cam., which parasitises the eggs of both moths, the percentage parasitism of those of N. c. capensis ranging up to 64, but it is itself attacked by unidentified secondary parasites. Endrin sprays gave good control of the moths [cf. 50 425; 53 421], but re-infestation occurred; the egg parasite was not greatly affected.
HEPBURN (G. A.). Review of forest and timber insects in South Africa (pp. 52-57). The author summarises information on insects attacking plantations of exotic trees in South Africa, points out the danger of establishing single exotic species over large areas, as has been done with Pinus patula, and emphasises the potential risk of introducing foreign pests; Sirex noctilio F., Urocerus (S.) gigas (L.) and Coptotermes amanii (Sjost.) have been intercepted in recent years.
THOMAS (E. D.). Cryptotermes brevis: control by methyl bromide (pp. 58-61). Cryptotermes brevis (Wlk.) appeared in Durban in 1918 and by 1947 had become a major wood-destroying insect there and in Port Elizabeth [cf. 38 302]. Timber preservatives are used for control, combined with replacement of damaged timbers, which is expensive, and fumigation with methyl bromide was tried. Fumigation at 3 lb. per 1, 000 cu. ft. for at least 24 hours, with initial fan circulation, proved effective, but protective sprays of pentachlorophenol and zinc naphthenate in oil are necessary to prevent re-infestation.
ANNECKE (D. P.). The parasites of soft brown scale (pp. 63-67). The soft brown scale [Coccus hesperidum L.] infests Citrus and other plants in many parts of the world. It has increased in importance largely as a result of fostering by ants and the use of modem non-selectivei insecticides [cf. 49 9], and much attention has been paid to its natural enemies, particularly in California. In South Africa, it is parasitized by 27 species of Chalcidoids, of which 16 are Aphelinids and nine are Encyrtids [cf. 53 472], though some of these are hyperparasites. The most numerous single genus is Coceophagus with 14 species, of which 12 are active parasites. Biological investigations on the mode of reproduction of the parasites are reviewed, and a note on predators is appended.
DE VILLIERS (W. M.). Die invloed van benseenheksachloried op die eier van die bruinsprinkaan, Locustana pardalina [The effect of BHC on the eggs of the brown locust, L. pardalina] (pp. 68-73). In experiments in South Africa, eggs of Locustana pardalina (Wlk.) exposed to technical BHC showed no effect until hatching, when the embryos died. When the four isomers were tested separately, α and β BHC had no effect, γ BHC killed the embryos just before hatching, and δ BHC was also effective, though less so than γ BHC, and furthermore had a delaying effect on development. When the isomers were used in pairs, combinations including γ BHC, even that with δ BHC, acted like γ BHC alone, but δ BHC with the α or β isomer showed the delaying effect. The α and β isomers together had no effect. The minimum effective exposure to BHC was to 0.0125 per cent. γ isomer for 12 hours. Further tests showed that BHC penetrated the eggs and that sensitivity was at a maximum when the eggs were in the first three days of development. Entry was mainly by way of the hydropyle. The mode of action is discussed.
MUNRO (H. K.). Trypetidae (fruitflies) in Africa (pp. 87-89). There are probably 1, 000 species of Trypetids in Africa and 300 in South Africa. The three main forms of feeding (in fruits, in flowers and seeds and in galls and stems) are reviewed, and species typical of each are mentioned, particularly those that have become pests.
COATON (W. G. H.). The national survey of the Isoptera (pp. 90-103). The author lists the 31 genera of termites known from South Africa and gives notes on mating habits and the distribution of individual species.
NEL (J. J. C.). Die groot grasdraertermiet in die Orange-Vrystaatstreek [The large harvester termite in the Orange Free State] (pp. 104-110, 3 refs.). Hodotermes mossambicus (Hagen) [cf. 48 62, etc.] is the most injurious insect pest of veld grazing lands in the Orange Free State; its bionomics, ecology and economic importance are discussed.
PRINS (A. J.). 'n Opname van die Formicidae in Suidelike Afrika [A review of the Formicidae of southern Africa] (pp. 111-116). The author gives notes on the classification and distribution of ants in South Africa, with information on the habits of some of them. All are indigenous, except Iridomyrmex humilis[Linepithema humile] (Mayr), which has become a pest of importance.
QUICKLEBERGE (C.). Entomological problems of the Eastern Cape (pp. 117-120). Notes are given on the principal insect pests of the Eastern Cape Province of South Africa, notably Colias electo (L.), a pest of lucerne, and on some useful insects that occur there.
VAN ARK (H.). 'n Oorsig van die insekteprobleem van die Karoo [A review of the insect problems of the Karoo] (pp. 121-124). Notes are given on the principal insect pests of pasture and other plants in the Karoo area of South Africa.
OOSTHUIZEN (M. L). The effect of methyl bromide fumigation on the viability of cereal seeds (pp. 128-131). Various cereal seeds were subjected to fumigation with methyl bromide at dosages ranging from 4 lb. for 24 hours to 1 lb. for 12 hours at temperatures of about 20-25°C. [68-77°F.] and 50-80 per cent. relative humidity, with fans operated during the first 30 minutes. The results indicated that wheat was very susceptible to fumigation injury, sorghum slightly more tolerant, maize somewhat tolerant, barley reasonably resistant and oats highly resistant, one variety showing significantly higher resistance than any other cereal variety tested. The length of exposure, the concentration of methyl bromide and the interval before germination was tested made little difference to the results.
MILNE (D. L.). Growth retardation by nicotine in the cigarette beetle, Lasioderma serricorne (pp. 136-137, 1 ref.). Lasioderma serricorne (F.) can survive on stored tobacco with a nicotine content as high as 8.25 per cent. in the presence of 5 per cent. powdered yeast; without yeast, development is retarded by 4 per cent. nicotine. Investigations showed that nicotine inhibits the vitamin-producing symbiontsi in the gut of the beetle [cf. 50 650]. This indicates that chemicals such as potassium sorbate, which suppresses yeasts in the gut of the beetle, may afford control [cf. 53 634, etc.].
LEA (H. A. P.). The population dynamics of the brown locust in Pretoria (pp. 162-168). This is a review of investigations carried out in South Africa on factors affecting the population dynamics of the brown locust Louisiana pardalina (Wlk.). The outbreak area of this locust has been found to cover 100, 000 sq. miles of country [cf. 47 174], but the exact points within this at which outbreaks arise are not known. Phase variation occurs, but intermediate forms are common. In this connection the author considers that Wellington's finding of active and inactive strains of Malacosoma pluviale (Dyar) in Canada [49 485-486] may throw light on activity differences in L. pardalina, in which activity varies from place to place and some solitary hoppers appear to be more gregarious than others. There is some evidence that the 'wildest' individuals in phase solitaria at low densities are potentially the most gregarious when forced to concentrate. The implications of this for control are indicated.
OBERHOLZER (J. J.). Biologic van die stinksprinkaan, Zonocerus elegans Thunb. [The bionomics of Z. elegans (pp. 169-172). Observations on Zonocerus elegans (Thnb.) in the Orange Free State showed that the females lay 1-3 egg-pods each, containing 20-90 eggs, which hatch in October or early November. The locusts pass through five nymphal instars and become adult at the end of March. Ovi-position begins soon after, and some of the eggs hatch early and give rise to a second generation, while others remain in diapause. Natural enemies of the eggs are enumerated. The distribution of Z. elegans and other species of the genus in Africa and Madagascar is reviewed.
NEVILL (E. M.). Controlling the elegant grasshopper, Zonocerus elegans Thnb. (pp. 173-175). The author reviews areas in South Africa where Z. elegans is a pest and possible methods of control. Parathion, dieldrin and carbaryl (Sevin [carbaryl]) are effective in baits, dieldrin, parathion and aldrin in sprays, and carbaryl, dieldrin, parathion and γ BHC in dusts, but insecticidal control is not promising under ranching conditions in the Kalahari. The difficulty of sampling populations in such areas is stressed.
WIESE (I. H.). Susceptibility of honeybees to some insecticide spray formulations used on Citrus (pp. 176-182). The author reviews the development of the chemical control of pests of Citrus in South Africa, where special measures to conserve natural enemies are not adopted, and states that honey bees have suffered from the phosphate insecticides now used. The bees are not required for pollination, and spraying therefore continues throughout the flowering period. The addition of oil to the sprays was found to reduce the toxicity of parathion to the bees [cf. 52 421].
SEARLE (C. M.). Beneficial insects-insecticides and Citrus (pp. 183-187). The author summarises the various deleterious effects that insecticides may have on predators and parasites of pests and the techniques that can be used to obviate them, and reviews progress achieved in South Africa towards the integrated control of Citrus pests. The toxicity of parathion, malathion, azinphos-methyl (Gusathion) and carbaryl (Sevin [carbaryl]) to the predacious Coccinellids Exochomus flavipes (Thnb.) and Cryptolaemus montrouzieri Muis, and to the parasite Aphytis sp. was found to be very high, but dimethoate (Rogor) was less harmful to the Coccinellids; furthermore, the materials had no repellent properties and were persistent in their effects. No protection was afforded by the addition of oil, except that it caused parathion to lose toxicity very rapidly. Wettable copper oxychloride used as a fungicide left a deposit that had a mechanical, possibly abrasive, effect on the parasite.
BEDFORD (E. C. G.). Recent biological control projects and the further application of biological control (pp. 188-195). The author summarises the work on the biological control of insects and some other invertebrate pests carried out in South Africa in 1891-1950 and more recent work in 1950-61, lists further possibilities and discusses the problems involved.
PETTY (B. K.). Insecticides and their registration (pp. 196-200). The regulations relating to the registration of insecticides for use in South Africa are outlined and discussed. [This long abstract has been truncated].

title: Notes on forestry in China.
abstract: Consists of two parts: (1) forestry and logging in Chekiang province; (2) forests and lumber in Kwangtung province. In Chekiang the Chinese, for sound economic reasons, are growing logs on a rotation of about 30 years, and over some millions of acres of mountainous country, sound forestry practice is being followed and there is an approximation to sustained yield with age gradations from 1 to 30 years. Soil erosion is practically non-existent in spite of steep slopes and high rainfall. Extraction is carried out by hand skidding of logs down the slopes to the rivers, in which the forest workers are very skillful. The Chinese in this province are adept in the use of round timber in all construction work and there is little call for sawn timber. Forests consist mainly of Pinus massoniana, Cunninghamia lanceolata, Bamboo and Cryptomeria japonica. Kwangtung is largely deforested but afforestation is in progress. KEYWORDS: Cryptomeria japonica \ Cunniinghamia lanceolata \ Insects pests \ trees aerial control \ Pinus massoniana \ China forests, silviculture and logging practice \ China

title: Experimental program on brush control in Brazilian pastures.
abstract: Tabernaemontana fuchsiaefolia appears to be controlled by monuron, doses as low as 3 g. being effective on individual trees. The most effective and economical of the phenoxy compounds for basal and stem applications to this species was 2% 2, 4, 5-T (8 lb. acid equivalent/100 gal. diesel oil). Low-volume foliar sprays from aircraft were not satisfactory; spraying to run-off appeared to enhance control and there is evidence that the liquid reaching the ground penetrates the soil and kills the budding zone of the roots. Pterogyne nitens, another pest, is also controlled by 2% 2, 4, 5-T in oil as a basal application on both topped and untopped plants. However, recent investigations suggest that 2, 4, 5-TP (1 lb./100 gal.) may be better than 2, 4, 5-T. Acacia polyphylla and A. paniculata were successfully controlled by aerial applications of 2, 4, 5-T ester (1 lb./4 gal. of diesel/water emulsion). Best results were obtained from applications in the wet season when new growth was apparent. Further studies on controlling mixed woody brush showed that best control was obtained by 2, 4, 5-T at 1-2 lb./acre in 4-5 gal. diesel-oil/water emulsions; 2, 4-D/2, 4, 5-T mixtures were not as effective as 2, 4, 5-T alone; application after the rains, when there is a flush of new leaves, was more effective than application in the cool dry season; it is important to avoid grazing sprayed areas too soon, so as to allow the grasses time to replace the brush, and it may be necessary to re-sow areas after spraying. The economics of these chemical treatments are also discussed. KEYWORDS: Acacia paniculata \ Acacia polyphylla \ protection forests \ Pterogyne nitens \ Tabernaemontana fuchsiaefolia \ woody weeds \ chemical control \ herbicides

title: Transactions of the IXth International Congress of Entomology, Amsterdam, August 17-24, 1951. Vol. I. The Congress, Plenary Sessions, Communications at the Sectional Meetings.
abstract: The first volume contains the text of the lectures given at plenary sessions and of most of the papers read at sectional meetings, and the second comprises papers forming parts of symposia. The subjects of these symposia include the physiological relations between insects and their food-plants, regulation of population density in forest insects, epidemiology of tropical and subtropical insects, and organic phosphorus and systemic insecticides. Of the papers in the first volume, those concerned primarily with insect pests occupy some 500 pages. Much of the information in many of them is of general character or has been noticed from other sources, but abstracts of some are given below. EL ZOHEIRY (M. S.) & ASEM (M. A.). The Chinese Jute or Hollyhock Moth Crociaosema plebeiana as an anticipated Cotton Pest, pp. 472-477. Crocidosema plebeiana Zell. has been recorded as a pest of cotton in Queensland [R.A.E., A 31 265] and Italy [35 420], but has been taken on this crop only twice in Egypt, where it normally feeds on hollyhock (Althaea rosea). In 1949 and 1950, it was found in the north of the Delta heavily infesting Chinese jute, Abutilon avicennae [cf. also 20 622], which was introduced into Egypt about 1920, but neighbouring cotton was not attacked. Galls were formed on the terminal shoots of the infested plants, which were retarded and stunted in growth, developed multiple branching, and produced short fibres and a reduced yield of seed. Species of Chenopodium, which are weeds in cotton areas, were also found infested in August 1951. On A. avicennae, eggs of Crocidosema were laid in clusters of three or four on the aerial parts of the plants, the larvae developed within communal galls, sometimes accompanied by larvae of Earias insulana, and development was completed in about a month. Three generations occurred on this food-plant during June-September, and earlier ones on hollyhock. In 1950, effective control was obtained by removing infested plant parts and burning the galls. Sprays containing BHC or DDT protected the young plants, and soaking the galls with carbon bisulphide destroyed the larvae within them. The use of A. avicennae as a trap, crop for the protection of cotton is recommended. FERRIERE (C.). Les parasites de Lyonetia clerckella, pp. 593-595. A list is given of 11 species of Chalcidoid parasites reared from fallen leaves collected in February in the Canton of Vaud, Switzerland, under apple trees that had been heavily infested in the previous year by Lyonetia clerkella. The four most numerous, which together constituted 76% of the total, were a species of Cirrospilus near S. vittatus, Omphale sp., Achrysocharis sp. and Tetrastichus xanthops. Kemner's conclusion that the fallen leaves harbour useful insects [11 612] is thus confirmed, and it is suggested that they should not be removed and should be protected from insecticidal sprays applied in winter. RIVNAY (E.). The Threshold of Reproduction in Insects and its Application to Agriculture, pp. 607-610. The threshold of reproduction in insects is defined as the temperature below which courting, mating and oviposition do not take place. Attempts to estimate it from the relation between temperature and the rate of oviposition or duration of the oviposition period proved unsuccessful, and it was therefore determined for several insects of economic importance in Israel by laboratory rearings under controlled conditions. The results are given for Clausenia purpurea, which is released for the control of a mealybug of the group of Pseudococcus comstocki on Citrus [31 441], Capnodis tenebrionis and C. carbonaria, which are pests of stone-fruit trees [34 369], and Ceratitis capitata [30 402], and it is pointed out that in each case the threshold of reproduction is above the threshold of development. The information obtained indicated that Clausenia will oviposit as late as November and as early as March (it was observed doing so in the field on a warm day in February), that the number of sprays applied against the adults of Capnodis can be reduced to two (one in early May against the overwintered beetles and another in mid-July against the first generation) and that Citrus fruits can safely be exported in autumn without risk of infestation by Ceratitis. ROZSYPAL (J.). Dyspessa ulula, ein Schädling von Zwiebelgemüse [D. ulula, a Pest of Onions and Garlic], pp. 656-659. In 1950, larvae of the Cossid, Dyspessa ulula, severely injured a field of garlic in southern Moravia. Apart from the damage caused by their feeding, the small surface wounds permitted the entry of various microorganisms. Adult flight occurred in May-June, and eggs were deposited on the basal parts of the plants. The larvae fed in the bulbs for about 5-6 weeks in June-August and overwintered in cocoons in the soil or in the feeding cavities. They spun fresh cocoons in spring and pupated, and the adults were ready to emerge by the end of May. All stages are briefly described. HASE (A.). Die Einbürgerung des Speisebohnenkäfers als Freilandschädling in Deutschland [The Establishment of Bruchus obtectus as a Field Pest in Germany], pp. 666-667. Bruchus (Acanthoscelides) obtectus has been known as a pest of stored beans in Germany for about 30 years. During the past ten years, increasing numbers of cases of field infestation have occurred, including some in central Germany [cf. 30 544], and the Bruchid must now be considered an established field pest there. Near Berlin in 1950, 703 adults had emerged by early November from 4966 beans picked 15 late August from an experimental plot of a climbing variety; 92% of them were from those on the lowest 3 ft. of the plants, and infestation was three times as heavy on the south as on the north side. Adults were observed in summer on the flowers at places where bumble bees had injured the calyx in search of nectar. BYTINSKISALZ (H.) & NEUMARK (S.). The Eucalyptus Borer (Phoracantha semipunctata) in Israel, pp. 696-699. Species of Eucalyptus, notably E. rostrata, are of considerable potential importance in Israel, but they are attacked by Phoracantha semipunctata [cf. 41 219] when five years of age or more. When standing trees are attacked, those less than 12 years of age are completely girdled and usually succumb, but older ones may survive, though rendered susceptible to subsequent infestation. The adults live for about 40 days in summer and 180 in winter. Oviposition ceases when the minimum night temperature falls below 15°C. [59°F.] and is resumed when it reaches 16.2°C. [61.2°F.]. Eggs are laid during March-November in batches of 10-110 under loose bark on standing trees and on the lower surfaces of logs in contact with the soil. The maximum number deposited by single field collected females in the laboratory was about 300. The threshold for complete development was 11.5°C. [52.7°F.], and the thermal constants for egg, larval and pupal development and for total development to 50% emergence were 47.5, 885, 525 and 1, 566.7 day degrees C. [85.5, 1, 593, 945 and 2, 802.6°F.], respectively. No effective parasites have been observed. The irregular distribution of the infestation suggests that susceptibility to attack depends on the physiological condition of the trees; almost all those in part of one grove were heavily attacked when irrigation water was diverted from them. CEBALLOS (G.) & ZARCO (E.). Sobre el desarrollo y combate en España de la Liparis monacha, en masas de Pinus silvestris de las Cordilleras de la Meseta Central [On the Development and Control of Lymantria monacha in Forests of Pinus sylvestris in the Central Plateau of Spain], pp. 705-707. (With a Summary in English.) Lymantria (Liparis) monacha increased considerably in stands of Pinus sylvestris in central Spain in 1950-51, and investigations showed that the larvae hatched in May and pupated in July. In a large-scale experiment on control in 1951, wettable-powder and emulsion sprays of BHC and wettable-powder sprays of DDT were applied from an aeroplane between early June and early July at 2.7 lb. BHC and 2.25 lb. DDT per acre. The treatments gave about 80% mortality in all. BHC was the more rapid in action, but DDT was the more persistent, especially when followed by rain. REGNIER (R.). Importance des degats de la mineuse du cambium du peuplier pour l'industrie du déroulage, pp. 711-714. The manufacture of cheese boxes, for which poplar is employed, is becoming of increasing importance in France. High quality wood is required, especially for the curved sides of the boxes, which are made from strips 0.8 mm. in thickness that readily break if the wood is weakened. A frequent cause of weakness is damage to the cambial zone of the trees by the mining larvae of an Agromyzid the identity of which is uncertain but which causes injury similar to that recorded in Austria for a species of Phytobia (Dendromyza) [cambii] on willow by Schimitschek, who also observed similar damage on poplar [24 49-50]. The injury is not detected until the bark is removed. The Agromyzid is especially abundant in marshy districts and attacks trees up to 12 years of age. Oviposition occurs apparently in June, and pupation about July-August, in the soil at the base of the trees. The adults emerge in the following spring and there is one generation a year. Control in the plantations appears difficult, but measures might be taken in the factories to detect damaged wood before manufacture. BLETCHLY (J. D.). A Summary of some recent Work on the Factors affecting Egg-laying and Hatching in Anobium punctatum De G. (Coleoptera-Anobiidae), pp. 728-734, 2 figs., 6 refs. The conditions that render wood susceptible to attack by Anobium punctatum (Deg.) were investigated in the laboratory in southern England. For experiments on oviposition, strands of coarse cotton were wound round wood blocks cut radially or tangentially in the direction of the grain and small strips of butter muslin were bound over the two ends with more cotton to form a site for the deposition of eggs; access to the sides was prevented by covering them with adhesive tape. It was found in this way that blocks of plywood (beech or pine) made up with blood glue or, to a less extent, hide glue, were preferred to those made up with a synthetic glue (urea formaldehyde). Dissection of females showed that they are capable of laying upwards of 60 eggs, though the numbers actually deposited usually averaged less than 28. The females spend much time in the exit holes, which they re-enter and in which some eggs are laid, and those collected early in the emergence period laid more eggs than those collected later. At 20°C. [68°F.], relative humidities above 56% had little influence on the duration of the egg stage, but it was somewhat prolonged and viability was decreased at lower humidities [38 263]; the relative humidity in dwellings is variable but normally exceeds 56% during June-August. Temperature had a greater influence, incubation being accelerated by high average temperatures. At 22°C. [71.6°F.] and 76% relative humidity, about half the larvae established themselves in sound wood, but rather more became established when the wood was partly decayed, especially in the case of beech, and development was more rapid in decayed than in sound wood. EL MISTIKAWI (Abd el Megid). Chemical Control of the Cotton Leaf Worm, Prodenia litura L., in Egypt, pp. 766-769. In tests in Egypt in 1948, dusts and sprays of DDT, a spray of DDD and a toxaphene dust were more effective against Prodenia litura on cotton than dusts and sprays of BHC, but combinations of DDT and BHC gave even better control, and plants treated with them showed increased vigour. similar tests in 1949, sprays containing 0.1% DDT gave complete mortality, and a dust of 10% DDT and 3% γ BHC only 95%, but the dusted plants were taller produced more flowers and gave a higher yield than any others, although a dust containing 5% DDT, 3% γ BHC and 40% sulphur, which gave 96.7% mortality, produced the highest number of bolls. In large-scale trials of a dust of 10% DDT, 3% γ BHC and 40% sulphur in 1950, larvae in all instars were killed within a few hours of treatment, infestation by Platyedra gossypiella was reduced from 49.1 to 33.7% by one application and to 1.6% by two, and the yield of seed cotton per acre was about 300 lb. higher from the treated plots than from plots in which the normal hand collection of the eggs had been carried out. In the discussion that followed, the author stated that the dust was applied at about 11-13 lb. per acre on young plants and about 15-17 lb. on older ones, and that the toxic effects persisted for 2-3 weeks. One treatment should be applied against each generation. ANKERSMIT (G. W.). Local Differences in Foodplants of Phaedonia inclusa, "the soyabean Beetle" (Col., Chrysomelidae), pp. 800-803, 3 figs. Phaedonia inclusa is a serious pest of soyabean in East and Central Java, where it was first recorded on this introduced crop in 1905, but has not been observed on it in West Java, Sumatra (with the exception of one locality on the south coast) or Malaya, although these countries are within its range. In the Experimental Gardens in Bogor, West Java, where soyabean is extensively cultivated, it occurs only on the green manure plants, Desmodium spp. and Pueraria phaseoloides. No morphological differences were found between beetles from Desmodium in West Java and soyabean in East Java, and they interbred freely. When beetles from Desmodium and from soyabean were confined with leaves of soyabean, D. ovalifolium and Pueraria, they preferred the food-plant on which they had been reared. Some larvae from Desmodium completed their development on soyabean; when the resulting adults were presented with the three food-plants, they fed on soyabean on the first day only and preferred Desmodium to Pueraria. Attempts in West Java to infest soyabean with beetles from Desmodium were unsuccessful, and soyabean adjoining a heavily infested field of Desmodium was not attacked, though the beetles migrated to Pueraria when the Desmodium was destroyed; there was no migration from Pueraria to soyabean, as occurs in East Java. In Central Java, no eggs or larvae were found on D. triflorum in a field in which Phaedonia was abundant, though there were many eggs, but no larvne, on the leguminous weed Flemingia lineata, the latter was not accepted in the laboratory by larvae reared on soyabean. It is concluded that P. inclusa was originally associated with plants such as Desmodium and Pueraria, but that a race adapted to soyabean evolved in Central Java about the year 1900 and subsequently spread to East Java, where soyabeans are widely cultivated; it was introduced into southern Sumatra about 1940, probably with cattle fodder. WEIDNER (H.). Die Ausbreitung der Termite Reticulitermes flavipes (Kollar) in Hamburg [The Distribution of the Termite, R. flavipes, in Hamburg], pp. 829-832. Infestation of structural timbers by Reticulitermes flavipes (Koll.) in Hamburg was first discovered in 1937, when injury was caused to a conduit of the municipal heating system [26 193]. Several further cases of damage were discovered in 1939-43 in timber in houses connected with, or not far removed from, the heating conduits, and a nest of the termite was found in 1951 in the cellar of a house that had recently been restored. The infestation must in the latter case have arisen while the building was in ruins. The timber damaged included oak and pine. GRANDORI (R.). Protection et desinfestation du blé emmagasiné par des substances non-toxiques, pp. 849-853. Large-scale trials showed that an Italian bentonite containing 26% silica and 65% mont-morillonite controls insects in stored wheat with which it is incorporated at a rate of 0.2% and maintains it free from infestation. Adults of Sitotroga cerealella, Tinea granella, Plodia interpunctella, Oryzaephilus surinamensis, Tenebroides mauritanicus, Calandra oryzae, Rhizopertha dominica and Lacmophloeus ferrugineus died within a few hours of treatment and those of C. granaria within a week. The larvae died following contact with the dust. The initial effect of the bentonite is repellent, but only insects from the superficial layers can escape and survive. In all cases, reproduction is prevented. Treating the walls with DDT to destroy insects that escape from the wheat proved satisfactory in the laboratory, and also in large-scale trials with the bentonite in farms and warehouses in Italy in 1949-51, details of which are given. The bentonite acts probably through a desiccating effect of the silica on the insects [cf. 38 306, etc.]; in addition, the particles penetrate into the joints, causing abrasions and eventually preventing the movement of the mouth parts, so that the insects starve. The montmorillonite probably absorbs water and so prevents the particles from agglomerating, even at relative humidities of 90%. Other advantages are the low cost of the bentonite and its harmlessness to man and domestic animals. It is most effective when used in moderately infested wheat of which the moisture content does not exceed 12.5%; when infestation is severe, the frass and débris from feeding cause the particles to agglomerate. title: 2,4-D residues in water following aerial spraying in a Scottish forest.
abstract: In preliminary studies to determine the consequences of using 2, 4-D in upland water catchments in Britain, an emulsifiable formulation of the nonyl ester of 2, 4-D at 4 Ib in 12 gal water was applied from the air over 150ac of Cairn Edward Forest, Kirkcudbrightshire on 25 August 1966. Samples of water from a drainage ditch were taken for analysis before, and 1 to 28 days after, spraying. Chromato-graphs showed that 2, 4-D residues increased from 1.5 to 2 ppm 1 to 4 days after spraying and decreased thereafter. No 2, 4-D was detected 28 days after spraying, indicating that all the free 2, 4-D ester had disappeared from the site. Very little rain fell in the first 10 days after spraying but over 5 in. fell in the following fortnight; therefore it was impossible to determine the minimum amount necessary to remove any traces of 2, 4-D not absorbed by the vegetation and peat soil, or the concn of 2, 4-D in the water draining from the peat.-M.Y.

title: Site classification [in the Canadian boreal forest].
abstract: In investigations in the north-eastern coniferous section of the boreal forest in Quebec, soil, vegetation and forest stands of 17 catchments were sampled. Analysis of the data obtained permitted classifications of the forest associations into 6 main types: (1) herb and fern forest; (2) herb and moss forest; (3) moss forest; (4) moss and dwarf-shrub forest. (5) peat moss and dwarf-shrub forest and (6) lichen and dwarf-shrub forest; with subdivisions according to predominant ground cover and tree species into some 30 sub-types. The first 3 main groups are upland types, i.e. are found on mountain slopes or on well drained lowland; (4) includes both rapid and poor drainage, both associated with slow-growing forest; (5) includes swamps not entirely deprived of drainage or in which the water-table is lowered during the growing season; (6) consists of slowly evolving sub-climax-types, probably resulting from fires, in dry situations. Results of investigations on 350 plots showed that the 6 associations and related site types formed 4 site classes of merchantable stands, with height of average dominant trees at 50 years for Class I 45-54 ft., II 35-44 ft., III 2534 ft., and IV 15-24 ft. On an average, 91% of the plots examined fell within the range assigned to their type: investigations in W. Quebec and a study of literature on N. Saskatchewan showed that these types would be applicable there. The possibility of classifying forest sites using air photographs and a minimum of ground work is discussed. The classification showing the related site-quality classes is given in tabular form. [Cf. For. Abstr. 14 (No. 2528). title: Effect of ammonium nitrate and lime on germination and early growth of Pinus caribaea.
abstract: Depressive effects of N salts on germination and survival of Pinus caribaea were investigated in the glasshouse by following changes in mineral soil N after the application of lime and fertilizers. The effect of partial soil sterilization was also studied. Immobilization and subsequent nitrification of NH4NO3 was greatly stimulated by liming. Germination and survival declined as the soil nitrate was raised, apparently because of increased pre- and post- emergence damping-off by Fusarium spp. Seedling yield at 20 weeks was reduced by high soil nitrate, but this effect was reversed at 31 weeks except at the highest nitrate content. The percentage of total N in seedling tissues was closely correlated with the concentration of soil nitrate. The effects of partial soil sterilization on plant behaviour were slight compared to those of NH4NO3 and lime. From authors' summary. title: Africa, a dying land. The degradation of African soils under the influence of colonization.
abstract: The author's aim is to give a comprehensive review of the alarming and accelerating soil degradation which has been taking place all over Africa south of the Sahara during the last hundred years, and which has coincided with the disturbance by European colonization of the delicate equilibrium between climate, soil, vegetation and indigenous systems of agriculture and society. He deals in greater detail with the Belgian Congo, but covers all the principal territories. In addition to about 100 authorities cited in the bibliography (mainly Belgian and English with some German), a great number of others are referred to in footnotes. The work is divided into 7 books, as follows: (1) The formation and maintenance of soils with particular reference to African conditions (69 pages). (2) The degradation of African soils, reviewing the present state of erosion, etc., in the principal territories, one by one (72 pages). (3) The causes of disequilibrium.burning, overstocking, agricultural methods and overcropping (50 pages). (4) The transformation of the native way of life by the colonists; with consideration of the spiritual, material, economic, political and social factors (74 pages). (5) The transformation of African agricultural economy by the colonists (110 pages). (6) The remedies for these evils (125 pages). (7) The application of the remedies (40 pages). The remedies are divided into three degrees (a) those directed against the symptoms of the disease, i.e. measures to stop the degradation of the vegetative cover, measures to improve the hydrographic regime, and measures to prevent accelerated erosion and soil degradation. Some 30 pages are devoted to afforestation and reforestation; (b) prevention of further injury by improving methods of stock raising and agriculture; (c) a complete revision of the attitude of the Europeans towards the land and the native population. title: Topographical forest typology.
abstract: The aerial survey of tropical forests reveals types based on topography, that were not clearly shown by ground surveys. As a result of visual reconnaissance and study of air photographs in Madagascar and West Africa, the author makes some tentative contributions to a typology based on topographical factors: (1) Dotted (tense forest ('foret dense tachetee') is very widespread in the middle ivory Coast, on the border between savanna and tropical forest. The peneplain is dissected by a network of gallery forest, and in the centre of each topographical cell bounded by the forest lines of the thalwegs, is a nucleus of forest isolated from the galleries by a border of savanna. (Three variations of this type are illustrated by air photographs.) This type of forest occupies the summits of small plateaux, while the surrounding slopes are savanna. (2) Digitate forest ('foret digitee') is the opposite to (1) in that it occupies the depressions in the relief, while the higher parts (hillocks, plateaux etc.) are savanna. it has a highly ramified appearance from the air, and is common in the middle and lower Congo and in parts of Madagascar, on plateaux dissected by recent erosion. (3) Wooded cirques ('cirques boises') are found where landslides occur on the slopes of hills, and the resulting cavities are rapidly filled with forest vegetation. This type of erosion is common on certain sedimentary soils in Gabon, middle and southern Congo, and especially in Madagascar. (4) Valley forests are widespread in the region of the Kalahari plateaux of the southern Congo, which are traversed from north to south by numerous parallel affluents of the Congo. The valleys, sometimes of a considerable width, are filled with forest, but it ends abruptly at the edge of the plateaux, which carry treeless savanna. (5) Ridge forest ('foret de cretes') occurs in Mayumbe, where the upper parts of the hills are covered with forest, leaving their bases bare. (6) Dotted open forest ('foret claire tachetee'), which occurs in the middle of the ivory Coast, resembles (1) but with open Isoberlinia/Uapaca instead of dense semi-evergreen forest. (7) Tiger scrub ('scrub tigre') is a curious vegetation pattern observed in the Sudan, Nigeria, Italian Somaliland and northern Arabia. It is a type of the pre-desert region and displays dark bands of shrubby vegetation alternating with light bands of bare soil, the bands curving inwards more or less, giving the appearance of contour lines traced on the ground. They are sometimes practically concentric. (8) Striped scrub ('scrub ride') was observed when flying over the Gold Coast between Accra and Takoradi. The littoral savanna was traversed by dark rectilinear bands, parallel to one another and oblique to the coastline, of varying width and discontinuous. They consisted of some kind of scrub. This pattern was quite different from (7). (9) Clump savanna ('savanes a boqueteaux') is a natural formation, where the forest vegetation is concentrated in little black spots when seen from all airplane, (10) Savanna with bushes on termite mounds ('savane a buissons sur termitieres') is common in Uganda. The grassy savanna is dotted with termite mounds, each of which is topped by a bushy clump of shrubs, creepers etc. Each mound is surrounded by bare soil and appears from the air as a black dot in the middle of a light-coloured ring. (11) Littoral scrub, when detached from the forest of the interior, forms a narrow band, following the coastline exactly. (12) Mosaic facies is common in forests recently occupied and in course of clearing. title: Soils, plants and farmers in West Africa.
abstract:
Relations between soil, vegetation and native farming in an area of forest/savanna mosaic with moderate rainfall in SW Nigeria are discussed, (a) on the basis of the first results from a study involving 8 miles of transects across savanna/forest boundaries on 13 sites, in which natural and cultivated vegetation and edaphic factors were recorded, and (b) on the basis of aerial photos. The most important observation was that there was a correspondence between the change from forest to savanna and changes in soil properties such as clay content and depth, which affect the ability of the soil to retain moisture and the development of root systems. Savannas emphasize and forests reduce the effect of seasonality of rainfall. Boundaries appeared to be stable under present conditions; a tendency to change, if any occurred, would probably involve an advance of the forest. Great variability of burning patterns in time and space, and a relation between soil factors and the effect of burning were noticed. Three crop systems (root/banana/tree crops, mixed maize/roots and small crops, and grain/roots) are examined in relation to the interplay of economic and ecological factors in the choice of crops and of forest or savanna sites for cultivation, and in relation to questions such as the higher yields obtained on forest soils versus the greater labour needed to clear the forest and weed. Where the tree rotation includes Cacao or Kola as cash crops, forest sites are essential. [Cf. FA 30 No. 1771.

title: Control of grasses and other vegetation in [conifer] plantations.
abstract: Discusses physical and economic effects of weeding, especially chemical weeding. It is seen chiefly as a moisture-conservation measure, but also as a means of reducing competition for nutrients, air temperature round seedlings, and animal activities in plantations. Soil-working methods and chemical methods before and after planting, with special reference to aerial application of atrazine at 3-5 lb. active principle/acre, are described. Establishment costs per 100 live Douglas Fir plants are tabulated for different spacings, planting methods and treatments (disking and/or atrazine, or none), showing that increased survival more than compensates for weeding and disking costs. [Cf. F.A. 27 Nos. 483, 5913.1. title: The scientific basis for conservation farming.
abstract:
Land-capability maps have been drawn by marking on aerial photographs the soil type, slope of land, erosion damage, wet and flooded areas, etc. The land is then divided into 8 classes.
Land suited to cultivation includes: (1) well drained land that can be cultivated safely with ordinary good farming methods; (2) good, gently sloping land that can be cultivated safely with contouring, cover-crops, rotations and water management. Moderate erosion is common; (3) land that can be cultivated safely with terracing and strip-cropping. Moderate to severe erosion is usual.
Land suited to limited cultivation comprises:
(4) land best suited to pasture and hay that can be cultivated occasionally for not more than one year in 6.
Land not suited to cultivation includes:
(5) level land suitable for grazing or forestry. There is little erosion; (6) land suitable for grazing or forestry, sloping and subject to water or wind erosion. It may have a high water table or hardpan. Fencing, limited grazing, contour furrows and water spreaders may be, needed to protect the land and increase grass growth; (7) land suitable for grazing or forestry but needing extra care to prevent erosion or damage from burning. This steep, rough land is highly susceptible to deterioration and must be grazed with extreme care; (8) land unsuitable for either cultivation, grazing or forestry; usually steep, rough, stony, sandy, wet or highly erodible.

title: Erosion in the Jumna and Chambal ravines.
abstract: A summary of the report of a United States Soil Conservation Officer who visited India in 1945, with comments by Indian Forest Service Officers. The main points of the report are: - (1) The area is so highly eroded that it should not be grazed at all. (2) The proper land use for these areas is fuel production. (3) Control of head erosion in these gullied areas cannot be accomplished by vegetation alone. The whole erosion system or unit must be dealt with and this includes a strip of the upper, level, cultivated lands which are the sources of water that cause the gullies. Cultivation should not be permitted near the overfalls at the head of the gullies and farther back a system of contour farming including contour bunding should be practised. (4) Control measures should include gully plugging. (5) Cultivation of steep and rough areas in the ravines should not be practised. Cultivation should only be attempted in the broad nullahs. Comments of I.F.S. officers on items (1)-(4) express general agreement with the report in theory, though they point out practical difficulties in Indian conditions. On item (5) they combine in condemning the suggestion that cultivation should be allowed in broad flat- bottomed valleys; cultivation is impossible in India without cattle, and it is agreed that no grazing is advisable in these areas; moreover, the broad nullahs are the most suitable sites for fuel plantations. title: Control of tea rots in Kivu.
abstract: The commonest pathogens are Rosellinia necatrix and Armillaria mellea. Rosellinia bunodes and Fomes noxius have also been observed. Preventive measures include felling the forest before planting tea and removing the tree roots from the soil, keeping the tea shade light by removing young trees or old dying trees, and removal of anti-erosion hedges of woody species such as Leucaena, which are regularly cut back and may form foci of infection. Direct control consists of the removal by excavation of diseased tea bushes showing symptoms in their aerial parts and of surrounding tea bushes showing infection of the collars and upper roots, complete with their root systems in both cases, and of the diseased parts of the roots of recently infected bushes adjacent to these.

title: The growth of conifers on prairie soils.
abstract: In 1871-72, six coniferous and 18 deciduous tree species were planted on heavy prairie.soil at the University of Illinois. Growth data are presented for Pinus strobus, P. sylvestris, Larix decidua and Picea abies. Planted trees grew very rapidly for the first 25-30 years and most heights were well above the average for Site I, but from 30 years onwards height growth in all cases fell below that of normal plantations on which the site indexes were based, and at age 70 their site indexes approximate to, or are even below, the lowest standard site index. Latitude is the primary climatic factor at Urbana. Weather statistics from 1888 show minimum and maximum temperatures of 25° F. and 108° F. respectively. Average annual precipitation is about 36 in. The average dates for the last killing frost in spring and the first killing frost in autumn are April 25th and October 15th respectively, leaving 180 frost-free growing days. The data, which are presented graphically, illustrate the errors which may occur in using site-index curves to classify young stands on areas different from those for which site-index curves were constructed. Observation and study of over 100 plantations on prairie soil throughout Illinois show that conifers in general follow the same growth pattern, while native hardwoods follow closely that of the normal plantations on which the site indexes were based. title: Exotic forest establishment on a recent volcanic soil.
abstract: An account of early stages in establishing a new exotic softwood forest for pulpwood on the Tarawera river, Kawerau, New Zealand, in 1961-63. The climate, vegetation, and soils are described. Methods of scrub clearing and their cost are given. Techniques used successfully to reduce vegetation before burning included crushing by (a) bulldozer, (b) 3-ton ball and chain between two tractors, or (c) 'tree stinger' (heavy tractor-mounted, 3-ton, 12-ft. steel boom with toothed head), and (d) chain-saw felling; (a) was used on scrub 6-15 ft. high, (b) on scrub 40-50 ft. high with average stem diam. 5 in., (c) on large stems, and (d) on steep ground. Tree poisoning with Ammate, As2O3, and cacodylic acid was unsuccessful. Consolidation of felled slash for ca. 12 months before burning is recommended, and a programme of clearing scrub and repeated burning of slash and weeds for 5 years before planting is suggested. Species planted in 1962 and 1963 were Pinus radiata (mainly) and Pseudotsuga taxifolia. Nursery and handling techniques are described. Planting was (1) by hand, using spades, on hilly or broken ground, and (2) by Lowther planting machines used singly, or in multiples, elsewhere; survival with (2) was best, exceeding 95%. Cost of (2) in 1963 was $4.10s. /acre. Aerial sowing by helicopter of fungicide- and bird-repellent-treated P. radiata seed in spring 1963 was only partially successful owing to high summer soil-surface temperatures; autumn sowing is recommended. The cost was <pounds>3 8s. 6d./acre. title: Aeration and other soil factors affecting Southern Pines as related to littleleaf disease.
abstract: In one series of pot experiments (A) with 1-year seedlings of Pinus echinata and P. taeda (one of each per pot) mixtures containing 5 or 21% O, plus 10% CO2 and the balance N, or compressed air, were forced through a sandy growing-medium; in another, (B), the seedlings were grown in 8 different soils from sand to clay under two watering regimes (moderate and heavy). Half of each treatment was inoculated thrice during the two years of the experiment with Phytophthora cinnamomii. In both (A) and (B), height growth was greater in P. taeda, and root growth in P. echinata. Oven-dry weight of aerial parts was similar for both. P. taeda produced relatively, and P. echinata presumably absolutely more mycorrhizas. Lack of O reduced growth but excessive CO2 alone did not; P. taeda was the less sensitive to poor aeration, but the more sensitive to poor nutrient conditions. In both (A) and (B), mycorrhizas were adversely affected by low O content, but high CO2 stimulated their development. P. echinata was more susceptible to Phytophthora cinnamomi. Low O content alone did not affect pathogenicity of the fungus but damage increased from the light to the heavier soils and from the moderately wet to the wet condition. title: Silvicultural effects of skyline crane and high-lead yarding.
abstract: Extraction of 77, 000 bd. ft. of logs per acre from steep slopes in the Sitka Spruce/Western Hemlock type in Oregon by aerial cableway (a), exposed 6.4% of the mineral soil, compared with 15.8% exposed by conventional high-lead skidding (b), though the difference was not statistically significant. Skidding trails with (a) tended to be across the slope, and with (b) up and down (more conducive to erosion). Damage to advance growth and other vegetation was about the same for both systems. Much less road construction is needed for (a) than for conventional systems. title: (1) Life history and distribution of Pythium and Rhizoctonia in relation to damping-off of Red Pine seedlings. (2) Influence of temperature, moisture, and soil reaction on the damping-off of Red Pine seedlings by Pythium and Rhizoctonia. (3) Seasonal development in the
abstract: These three papers deal with two important damping-off fungi, Pythium irregulare and Rhizoctonia solani. The first paper is concerned chiefly with the identification of the two fungi, the symptoms they induce, their life histories in relation to pathogensis, and their distribution. The second and third papers give the results of studies on the influence on the fungi of certain environmental factors. Both fungi are responsible for serious damping-off among seedlings of Red Pine (Pinus resinosa) in forest nurseries in Wisconsin. The symptoms which they induce in the host vary according as they are associated with other organisms and appear to be correlated with their respective growth habits. Temperature had a marked effect on the virulence of both fungi. At 33°C. about half of the seedlings were killed by Pythium, while at 12° C. more than 90 per cent. were killed. Only a few seedlings were killed by Rhizoctonia at 12°, but losses increased to a maximum of 58 per cent. at 24-30° and decreased again at 33°. Damping-off by Pythium increased with the moisture content of the soil from a minimum at 13 per cent. to a maximum at 100 per cent., while that caused by Rhizoctonia gradually increased from a high level at 13 per cent. to a maximum at 68 per cent., and then decreased to a minimum at saturation. Air humidity has little or no effect on the amount of damping-off by Pythium, although losses caused by aerial mycelia of Rhizoctonia increased with high air humidity. Acidity was found to be an important factor affecting the growth of both the fungal mycelia and the Pine seedlings. Conditions most favourable to damping-off by Pythium were between pH 5.2 and 8.5. This range was only moderately favourable for Rhizoctonia but mycelia development of that fungus was rapid with acidity stronger than pH 5.2. Increase in damping-off by both fungi at levels above pH 7 appeared to be associated with decline in host development. The highest percentage of damping-off occurred in Red Pine seedlings about 11-13 days old. After about a month the seedlings became comparatively resistant to the disease. KEYWORDS:

title: Undesirable woody plants [6 reports.
abstract: Experiments in the selective control of Artemisia tridentata growing among Purshia tridentata showed the former's greater susceptibility to 2, 4-D or 2, 4, 5-T (3 lb./acre); P. tridentata was more susceptible to 2, 4, 5-T than to 2, 4-D. Both species were most susceptible to June spraying. Kill of A. tridentata by aerial spraying with 2, 4-D was greater at 10 ft. than at 50 ft. Comparisons of (a) areas showing 100% control of A. tridentata with (b) untreated areas, revealed that (a) held snow longer in spring than (b), and the soil moisture at 18-19 in. depth in June, July and Aug. in (a) was 63.8-30.2% greater than in (b). By May 1959 good control of Quercus douglasii had been given by 2, 4-D PGBE (propylene glycol butyl ether ester) at 2 lb. acid equivalent (a.e.) applied in May 1955 and 1957, and by 2, 4, 5-TP PGBE at 2 lb. a.e./acre applied in May 1955, June 1956, and May 1957; similar control of Rhus diversiloba was obtained by 2, 4, 5-TP PGBE at 2 lb. a.e./acre applied in May 1955, 1956 and 1957. The 2, 4, 5-TP (acid) treatments killed some Q. wislizenii; 2, 4-D and 2, 4, 5-TP PGBE at 2 lb. a.e./ acre were much less effective on this species, and damaged Pinus sabiniana. In general, the effectiveness of follow-up spraying was greater in May than in June. Amine formulations were ineffective. The % kill of Tamarix spp. in Oct. 1959, after treatments in July 1958 at 20 lb./acre, was: fenuron 88%, monuron 47%, polychlorobenzoic acids 42%, 2, 3, 6-TBA 53%, and 2, 4-D (acid) 45%. The rates of fenuron affecting Q. turbinella were 8 lb./acre (old and mature plants), and 16.64 lb./acre (coppice growth on burned areas).
